speaker: interviewee
text: May 1995. Morris, you were just saying that you had offer from Polish friends to hide. Yes, but my-- they were saying my parents didn't want to go. So we had to make a quick decision because it was really very bad. We knew that the Germans will come any-- any time now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we digged a very big hole in the garden there-- quite, quite big and with shrubs all around. We put blankets there and food for about a week or so. We were hoping that the Germans will come, and after a day or so, whatever they will do, we be able to come back and see our parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But within the last minute, it didn't take long-- a day or so-- and the Germans came in, and we could hear shooting going on. And our next-door neighbor came and told us, look, better get away because the Germans are already here, and most probably you can hear the shooting. So we went still with our parents to the hiding. They were there, my parents and one sister with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many of you were there? There was you, your parents, and a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one sister went-- because this sister had very good papers that she was working for the Germans, and we were sure they won't do anything to her. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But where did the others go?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what's coming to, yes. So we took our parents there, and you can imagine. And then we said goodbye to them, and we-- we run away. And we run to this Pole-- the one who promised us that he keep us in case something will happen. On the way, we could see hundreds of people running everywhere, people that we knew from the city, and no one could do anything to help them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we arrived there, and he took us in, the Pole. He took us in, and while we were there, we begged the gentleman if he could do something about our parents because we were worrying very much. So he said, look, tomorrow morning I'm going to the city, to Jaworszice. And if they agree to come, I would bring them. So we were so happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when he came back, [SOBBING] we knew that they're not alive anymore, because when you looked at his face, you could read, and you could-- you could see that something has happened. So he told us the bad news, that our parents were killed there, and my sister. But first-- first my parents were killed. And then-- and then my sister was still alive but she-- instead of running away from the parents, she was still lying with the parents. She was just hysterical. She didn't know what to do. [SOBBING] And then the Germans came and took her, and from what we know, I don't know if they shot her or she-- she went. Exactly I really can't say 100% because I really don't know. But from what I heard, that she was shot dead. But we don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this-- this is how I lost my parents and my sister. That was the start. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in the hiding then. We were there for-- for a little while. And the Germans were very clever because they knew the Jews hiding somewhere. So they declared an amnesty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- we were not sure if this is just a trick or something and they will get us and kill us all. But they-- no, the-- what we heard is that everyone can go back to their homes, and everything will be all right. Anyhow, we took the risk, and we went back home. We went back home because we had there everything. So we decided that we will sell. Whatever is there we will sell. We were not allowed to do it, but there were plenty of Poles that wanted to buy, especially goods like we had. They-- they-- everyone wanted to buy. And we sold whatever we had in the place. And then we tried to make Polish documents for us and every one of who had a Polish document. But they were just-- they were not 100% because you couldn't be registered anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the risk you had to take. But we paid a lot of money. The underground was making those things. We paid a lot of money. And then the time came that we had to go back because the Germans-- we could see what's happening again. They're going to clean everyone out from the city. So-- Where were you at this stage with your false-- you had false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were just having them with us, the papers-- if something have happen so that we--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were still at home--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still at home, yes. But we had the papers always with us. If something happen, we can run away. And this is what had happened. My sister was already-- wasn't home already. Two of my sisters were not home anymore. That's right. They went to Krakow. And the sister that I've got now, which is here in Sydney-- she was-- till the end of the war, she was a maid working for someone. And she lived through the war. But she had a very hard time because with the Poles, it wasn't so easy, and she was a maid for the Poles. And sometimes they recognize that she was a Jewess, and in the middle of the night, she ran away. And then she looked for another job. So she didn't have anybody. But anyhow she survived. And we-- we-- when the time came, we run away. We had the documents, and we run back to the hiding, to this Pole. And we been there for couple of months or so. We give him everything that we had-- jewelry, suits, whatever we had-- money-- we give him. But certainly, we still got a bit of money left with us in case of an emergency so that we be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Novak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Novak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I even remember the place at [NON-ENGLISH]. The-- the-- on the land, it was. And we gave him everything that we had except some money that in case in emergency so that we have he didn't know about it. But we still have-- had suits left in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he have done it without the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't be able to survive. Without the money you couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he have helped you without the payment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not at all. He knew we had it-- money-- and he knew that he can get lots of things out from us. They were very nice people, especially him. He was a lovely person. But his wife knew that she can get a lot out from us. And it happened so that after couple of months, they used to come to us and said look, we can't keep you anymore. It's getting risky and so and so. But we know that it was Mrs. Novak and not him, that she's got enough. She knows she's got everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't know what to do. But we had still when we run away, we couldn't take all the suits with us. So we hid it in a box, hidden. Maybe 20 or 25, I don't remember-- suits, new suits. We hid it in a shed, and it was covered with wood. And when-- when he came, we told him, look, Mr. Novak, we still have something in Jaworszice, and we will try to go and get it. And he ask us, how will you go? And we said, we will risk our life. We will go, and we'll get it. Because he didn't know where it was, and he couldn't get it. So in-- in the hiding, I was-- the oldest brother was there, and there was the youngest sister and the other brother. So-- and there were no Jews anymore anywhere. Jews were only in ghetto or in concentration camp. You couldn't find a Jew anywhere else. So you had to take a-- a terrible risk to go. We had the papers, but as I was saying, the papers as they so long ago write till a Pole or someone didn't know you crying, didn't-- because they knew those papers are no good. So first my sister, the one who is here, asked the older brother if he would go. What were their names? We should give them their names-- your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Celina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother said didn't he want to go. So she asked the younger brother, and he said, no, he was afraid to go. I could see that no one really wants to go. I don't know why. I said look, Cesia, if you want to come with me, I know-- I know the road. I know because we used to go to the people during the German occupation to the country. And I said, I know the way. And I said, I go with you. She was afraid at first, but then she said all right, because otherwise, we all had to go. And we went. Didn't take long-- half an hour, half an hour, maybe 3/4 of an hour after we left Mr. Novak. There was a Pole there who recognized me. He didn't recognize my sister, but he recognized me because he most probably used to come to our place to buy suits.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I begged him to let me go, and he said-- I said to him that I am not Jewish. He said, you-- how can you say you are not Jewish? You live there and there. So he knew exactly where I lived, so I couldn't say anything. So I was-- I could see what the end is going to happen. And he was screaming, I get for you a kilo of sugar, and I will watch you being killed. This is-- this is a peasant. And I could see that it was very bad, so I whispered to my sister, Celina-- I whispered to her, look, can you see the cross-- across the road? Because I knew the area very well there. And she said yes. I said, look, you run there, and then when I see that you are already there, I will start running, because I could run better than she could. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I saw that she was there already, I start running because he was with a horse and a buggy on two-- two wheels. And I run away, but he was after me with the horse, and he start-- the horse start jumping. And when I-- when I was running, he start screaming. And there were little huts from Poles living all around on the land. And they came out with knives, and they stopped me. When they stopped me, I start screaming, Jesus, Mary, I don't know what he wants from me. He's drunk. They looked at me, and they put their hands down. And when I could see this-- and they said, I don't think he's Jewish. I don't know what he wants from.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I run to my sister. I run to my sister, and then, by the time he could stop his horse and hold it, I was already at my-- with my sister, and we run into the forest. And my sister said to me, I like to die-- look how you look. I was like Snow White because I was so scared because I knew they're going to kill me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lie down for a little while because I couldn't walk anymore. When we had a bit of a rest, we went, and after we arrived to Jaworszice, that was at night already. And as we were walking, we went, like, from here to next street here, Victoria Road. It was a cemetery before our house, not far, because we live next to a church. And there were lights everywhere because someone maybe notice there's-- or thinking that someone is walking, so they were pointing with their spotlights on us. And I said to my sister, look, it's not far from our home. Let's crawl. We crawl through the cemetery, and this way, we might be able to arrive without any problem. And this is what we did. We crawled right through, and we came in. We came in, and I had to go into my neighbor, to the landlady where we lived, and to tell her, because otherwise I couldn't get out the stuff where I wanted to get it out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as we knocked at the door and she saw us, she nearly dropped dead. She was so scared, because even-- even a Pole, if he would take in a Jew, he would get killed for it as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I could see this, I just closed the door, and I went to the next-door neighbor. What was his-- I can't remember the name. I-- I-- and the next-door neighbor, they didn't worry at all. They took us right in. They had 13 children. And they were very strict Catholics.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us in, and they gave us a cup of tea, and we settled down a little bit. And they had a son-- Janek was his name. And after a while-- didn't take long-- the landlady came in because she was jealous that she didn't take us in and the other people did. And then we had another neighbor who was a doctor, and when they heard us coming, they came into the-- those other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were there for about half an hour. We had a bit of a rest. We had a drink. She wanted to give us to eat, but we didn't want to eat anything because we couldn't eat, really. And we told them what we here for. We explained to them that we-- we didn't tell them where we are, because you never know what can happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were telling them that we in the forest, and we haven't got any money or anything. And we've got still here some parcel, and I would like to take this parcel. They said, no problem at all. And this Janek, he said, look, Maury, I will take you right through. I take you through the cemetery and see if everything is all right. I let you go then. And that's what he did. He went with us past the cemetery. It was quite late already at night, and-- and everything was all right. We went right through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking for an hour or so, and then my sister says, can you hear a noise? And I says, no. After a little while, they were coming closer, and they were-- I could hear the noise. And I said, look, but this way, a horse or a car can't come this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: But unfortunately, it was a horse, and it was a buggy, and a German was on it. And there was a doctor who lives not far from our place. And he knew who we were. He knew everything. And the German stopped us. And when they were coming closer-- before when they were coming closer, I said to my sister, you know, I don't know who they are, but they're going to kill us. Because even Poles were killing you just to get whatever from you, whatever you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when they came closer up, and the German stopped, and he said, what are you doing here? And we could see the doctor in the thing. And he was very nice. He didn't say anything-- not a word. And the Germans-- they most

speaker: interviewee
text: probably-- the German must have taken this doctor to somebody. And the German, when he asked us what are we doing, and we told him that's the best time of the night. We just went for a walk. And the-- the-- the Pole, the doctor, said, well, they just youngsters. They just trying to have a good time. And they let us go. And then we came into the hiding. We came into the hiding. It was quite late at night. And in-- in the morning, Mr. Novak came to see if we arrived because they didn't know at night. We didn't bother them. And we gave him all those things. And unfortunately, it didn't take much longer-- another couple weeks of-- or months or so-- and he came in. He said he can't help us any longer. He knew that's it, we-- we don't have anything else. I would say most probably it wasn't Mr. Novak. It was Mrs. Novak because he was a lovely gentleman, really nice, good-hearted man. And we had to decide to go. But earlier, as I mentioned, my sister was already in Krakow. She left from there. She left to Krakow, and she had a position already and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Celina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Celina, and Andzia as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andzia too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I was there with my-- and the older brother was up at the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I had to go, and they all went. And I had to go. I was only there with my-- with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the one who was in the camp with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called him [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And Mr. Novak-- Mr. Novak came in, and he said, look, I'm sorry but, you know, they're burning houses and things. If they find out, then they will kill us. We could see there is nothing to do anymore. We let my sister know that we coming, and we left Mr. Novak. And we had to take a risk. But we had our papers. But it still a-- it was a risk. We came-- we arrived to Krakow, because that's where they were, in Krakow. My sister was waiting at the station. And-- but she couldn't take us to her place because she was as a maid there, and she wouldn't want anyone to know anything. So we slept at the station the first night, and we didn't have where to go. We were only hoping that if there would be-- the Germans would-- you know, how they were taking people, Polish people, to Germany. If we would be together, maybe some miracle would happen. But unfortunately, we didn't have where to go. So we knew that there is a camp in Krakow. It's unfortunate-- it's unbelievable that being a free person, and then you want to go into a camp, to a concentration camp. But we didn't have where to go. That was the only place where we could go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the [NON-ENGLISH] in Krakow, and that's where they were working. Their camp was in Plaszow, but they were working every day. They were going out to the [NON-ENGLISH]. So when we came there, we met someone who was in the camp from our city. What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I really don't remember. But he-- he was in the camp for quite a while there already. And at first, he said that it's impossible. But then he tried very hard, and he managed to do something, everything for money. Without money, there was nothing to be done. And we managed to go into the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I found myself in a concentration camp with my brother. That was my first concentration camp. But I was quite happy, to be honest, because at Mr. Novak, we were in this-- there was a shed there with straw. And he had a big hole, and we were in this hole there covered with straw. Well, there was-- only you could go out at night if you need to do toilet facilities or something, you went out at night. But during the day, bad luck. So what was the camp like?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- I was happy to be in the camp, really, and I went to work. I didn't realize what-- what's behind me, but I was happy. I could see people going to work, and I was young. I was quite happy. But unfortunate, it wasn't so. After a few days, the Ukraines [? through ?] our guards. They used to come to the barracks, and just for no reason whatsoever, took you out and on spot they kill you. Just for no reason whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in front of me. They could have taken out someone from my bed and just done and kill you on the spot. And that was going on week after week. I can say even day after day they were killing people, just for no reason whatsoever. They were just taking out from the bunkers and kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was that like for you, a young boy, to see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what could I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like-- like-- I felt like an animal. I wasn't human anymore. I could see what was going on in front of me. I couldn't-- I couldn't scream, because if I would scream, I would be shot too. I couldn't say anything. So that was my first camp, and I went to-- I was going to work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what physical surroundings were like in the camp? Like how many of you slept in the one room? What sort of beds, that sort of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beds were not too bad. They were-- they were two-story bed, and there were two people in each bed. So there were four people. Look-- that-- that was quite good for-- according the way I had it at Mr. Novak, well, I didn't sleep on a bed at all. There were mice running all night on your body, you know, at Mr. Novak's, because there were lots of straw there, and the mice were in the straw. So they were just running around. But I was quite happy in the camp. I was-- in this way. But sleeping-- there was no problem at all. The only thing that you'd never knew. You could have been happy in a minute, and a minute later, you would have been shot. And the food didn't worry me at all because if you didn't have enough, you can always buy something when you went to work. You could give a Pole money, and they would buy you a-- there were Poles there, and they brought things. They made profit-- good profit, and they brought you things. And while I was in the camp there, my sister used to come sometimes and with food too. She used to come where I was working there as a Christian. She was on Christian papers like-- and she used-- but I begged her not to come. And then on the end, she-- she didn't come anymore because it was too risky. I begged her not to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the-- the-- the camp. But unfortunately, what happened a typhus came to the camp, and everyone was sick. And people-- people were dying day after day, by the dozens, you know, people were dying. So my time came, and I got sick. So I had the typhus. And while I had the typhus-- I don't know why, I even sometimes discuss this with my son-- but I-- I got blind. I was blind. I didn't see at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, they-- that was a hospital. But you can imagine the facilities in the hospital. They had some bugs in the bed that-- that-- that-- they were eating you up alive. So [NON-ENGLISH] was our boss in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he walked into the camp while I try to go down from the bed. And he saw me, and I didn't see. I was blind, completely blind, and I couldn't see. I was just going down. Maybe I needed to go to the toilet or something, and I wanted to go down. And as I was going down, he walked into this barrack. It was probably to kill someone or two, you know, because he didn't just come for no reason. And he saw me getting down from bed. And he came to me, and he says, what are-- what are you doing? And I said, I need to go down. And I said, I can't see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know why. It's just unbelievable that a German who killed everyday someone and used to use [? sport-- ?] if someone had a mustache, he asked him to go a few yards away, and he would start shooting, right through his mustache. That's the kind of a person he was. And he ask me my name. And he said to me, look, don't worry, I will send you something here. He called the fellow who was in charge, like a nurse in the hospital. And he said, I'll send you a bottle here of medicine for this boy. And not only did he send the medicine, he sent a loaf of bread with it as well. I just couldn't believe it. I just couldn't believe it because I-- I was with him for quite a while, and I knew the things that he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was out of character.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I just don't understand why. I can't understand. And this is what he did. So it took another few days or so, and I tried to get-- I felt a bit better, and I could see already. I used to take this medicine. I don't know what it was. They used to give me this medicine, and I felt better. But mainly, it was amazing. Used to-- when people got sick on typhus, when healthy boys went in, they came out. Then it was a time when you were a bit skinnier. You just had no chance of surviving. And there were different times. Unfortunately, my brother got sick after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When he got sick, I said to him, look, try to get out from the hospital as soon as possible because there you can't survive. With those bugs and things, it's impossible to survive. I was going to see him every day in the hospital, when I-- from work in the morning and then at night. And everything was all right. [SOBBING] One morning I went in, and I could see he's feeling every day a bit better. I was quite happy. And then when I came back at night from the camp, from the-- from work, he was dead. And what I found out afterwards that they injected all the people that they were in the hospital with methylated spirit. And the doctor's name was Cohen, a Jewish doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, you had-- what-- what I was told is that the Germans give them an order to kill so-and-so many Jews. So instead of taking healthy people, they took those who were in the hospital and injected them with the methylated spirit. This is what I was told. [SOBBING] And this is how I lost my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was alone. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have a break. End of tape 2.
